topic: An artist discovers someone has tattooed their work
raw_output:
[story 1]

The gallery lights felt like physical blows, illuminating the canvas where my soul had bled onto linen. I stood back, arms crossed tight against my chest, a sour knot tightening in my stomach. Then I saw it. A faint, almost invisible line tracing the curve of my most emotionally raw piece, a deliberate, stark addition that felt like a profound violation. My breath hitched, a sharp, shallow intake of air. I moved closer, my shadow falling over the spot, and the realization hit me with the cold shock of ice water. Someone had marked my creation. A slow, burning resentment began to coil in my chest, hot and suffocating. I didn't speak; I simply stared, my jaw clenched so hard my teeth ached, willing the intruder to acknowledge the sheer audacity of their trespass.

[story 2]

"You absolute charlatan," I hissed, the words scraping my throat raw. I circled the sculpture slowly, my boots making deliberate, heavy sounds on the polished concrete floor. My gaze locked onto the intricate pattern woven into the texture of the bronze—a signature, undeniably, yet utterly unwelcome. A wave of intense displeasure washed over me, making my skin prickle with irritation. I felt a deep, visceral urge to dismantle everything in my sight. I reached out, my fingertips hovering inches from the mark, trembling slightly with restrained fury. "Who authorized this desecration?" My voice was low, dangerously steady, each syllable laced with contempt, a clear warning that this unwarranted claim would not be ignored.

[story 3]

I watched the assistant approach the display, a sickening sense of unease settling over me. My entire posture shifted; I became rigid, every muscle coiled, radiating a palpable tension. When the assistant leaned in, I saw it—a small, almost hidden symbol etched into the lower corner of my latest oil painting. A deep, ugly churning started in my gut, a sensation akin to bile rising. I didn't need to ask. The sheer presumption of ownership over my expression made my vision narrow. I turned my back on the crowd, walking toward the artwork as if it were contaminated, my steps sharp and unforgiving. When I finally stopped before it, I didn't touch it, but the silence I commanded around me spoke volumes about the profound displeasure radiating off me.
